If she was 12 in 1776, she was born around 1764, making her only 17 at death in 1781 - not the 25-27 years old she should be based on the Edgar courtship timeline. This represents a significant 8-10 year discrepancy in Catherine's age at death.

issues.title: Hareton's age progression inconsistency
issues.description: Hareton is born in Chapter VIII during Frances Earnshaw's childbirth, which occurs after Catherine Earnshaw's marriage to Edgar but before her death. Young Catherine is born in Chapter XVI on the exact night that Catherine Earnshaw dies in childbirth. The timeline establishes that Catherine Earnshaw dies shortly after giving birth to young Catherine, yet Hareton was already born and presumably some age when this happened. However, the math doesn't work: if they were born at different times with Hareton being older, he should be much more than 5 years older, or if Catherine Earnshaw's death/young Catherine's birth happened shortly after Hareton's birth, they should be closer in age. The consistent 5-year age gap (18 vs 13, then 23 vs 18) suggests the author intended them to be 5 years apart, but this contradicts the established timeline of their births relative to Catherine Earnshaw's death.

issues.title: Heathcliff's mysterious wealth and education
issues.description: Heathcliff leaves as a degraded, uneducated foundling in Chapter VII and returns in Chapter X as a wealthy, refined gentleman with military bearing who can gamble large sums and eventually owns both properties through mortgage debts. The transformation occurs in just 3 years (he was gone from approximately 1778 to when he returns 'three years later'). While Mrs. Dean explicitly states she doesn't know how he acquired his wealth or education, this represents a significant unexplained transformation that strains credibility. The mystery may be intentional for Gothic effect, but it remains an unexplained change in the character's circumstances and abilities.

issues.title: Property inheritance legal inconsistencies
issues.description: The text states that Edgar altered his will to put Catherine's inheritance in trust to prevent it from falling to Heathcliff if Linton died. However, Heathcliff still manages to claim both the moveable property (through Linton's will) AND the lands through 'his wife's right.' This doesn't make legal sense because: 1) If Catherine inherited the lands directly from Edgar, they would be her separate property, not subject to her husband's will; 2) If Edgar put her inheritance in trust, Heathcliff shouldn't be able to claim it through marriage rights; 3) The phrase 'his wife's right and his also' is legally meaningless - Heathcliff can't claim property through both his son's inheritance AND his daughter-in-law's marriage rights simultaneously. The narrator's uncertainty ('I suppose legally') suggests even Brontë recognized this was problematic, but the plot requires Heathcliff to gain control of both properties for the story to work.

"He had bequeathed the whole of his, and what had been her, moveable property, to his father: the poor creature was threatened, or coaxed, into that act during her week's absence, when his uncle died."
"He determined to put it in the hands of trustees for her use during life, and for her children, if she had any, after her. By that means, it could not fall to Mr. Heathcliff should Linton die."

issues.title: Lockwood's illness duration vs. story length
issues.description: Lockwood explicitly states he has been ill for 'four weeks' and is too weak to read, requiring regular medicine every twenty minutes. Yet during this brief recovery period, Nelly Dean manages to narrate an extraordinarily detailed multi-generational saga spanning approximately 30+ years (from Heathcliff's childhood arrival through multiple deaths, marriages, and complex character developments). The sheer volume of dialogue, intricate plot details, character psychology, and chronological events she recounts would realistically require many weeks or months to tell, not the few days of conversation suggested by Lockwood's illness timeline. Additionally, Nelly supposedly has ongoing household duties during this period, making such extensive storytelling sessions implausible. While this is a common issue in frame narratives, it represents a structural weakness in the novel's realism.

evidence.quote: Four weeks' torture, tossing, and sickness! Oh, these bleak winds and bitter northern skies, and impassable roads, and dilatory country surgeons! And oh, this dearth of the human physiognomy! and, worse than all, the terrible intimation of Kenneth that I need not expect to be out of doors till spring!
Mr. Heathcliff has just honoured me with a call. About seven days ago he sent me a brace of grouse—the last of the season.

issues.title: Ghost Catherine's timeline contradictions
issues.description: Catherine's books show inscriptions from 'a quarter of a century back' (25 years ago), while her ghost claims to have been 'a waif for twenty years.' This suggests Catherine lived for approximately 5 years after writing in those books as a child before dying and becoming a ghost. However, this is not necessarily an inconsistency - it's actually consistent with what we learn later: Catherine wrote in the books as a child after her father's death, lived to adulthood, married Edgar Linton, and died in childbirth. The 5-year gap represents her transition from childhood (when she wrote the diary entries) to her death as an adult. The issue is more about unclear exposition than actual contradiction, making it a 'suggestion' rather than an 'error' - the timeline works, but could be clearer to readers.

characters.name: Catherine Earnshaw
characters.description: The passionate and wild daughter of Mr. Earnshaw who becomes the central figure in the novel's tragic love story. She is torn between her deep spiritual connection to Heathcliff and her desire for social respectability through marriage to Edgar Linton. Her death in childbirth becomes the catalyst for Heathcliff's decades-long revenge.
characters.physical: Bonniest eye, sweetest smile, lightest foot in the parish, bonny hair that becomes brown ringlets after her stay at Thrushcross Grange, with wonderfully whitened fingers. Later described as having hair flying over shoulders during emotional fits, eyes flashing, muscles standing out. In death, appears with sweet smile and angelic beauty.
characters.personality: Wild, wicked, mischievous, high-spirited, bold and saucy, defiant, sometimes affectionate, but also haughty, contemptuous, proud, dramatic, and manipulative. Torn between two worlds and two natures - civilized and savage.
characters.background: Daughter of Mr. Earnshaw, raised at Wuthering Heights alongside Heathcliff. Spent five weeks at Thrushcross Grange being 'civilized' by the Lintons. Married Edgar Linton but never forgot her spiritual bond with Heathcliff. Dies giving birth to her daughter.

relationships.description: Claims 'I am Heathcliff' - their souls are identical
Married for social status but loves like 'foliage in the woods' - temporary
Often in conflict due to his cruelty toward Heathcliff
Troubled relationship due to her wild behavior, but makes peace before his death

characters.arc: Represents the conflict between nature and civilization, passion and duty. Her choice to marry Edgar while loving Heathcliff sets the tragedy in motion. Dies torn between two worlds, never achieving peace in life.

characters.name: Heathcliff
characters.description: The dark, mysterious foundling brought to Wuthering Heights who becomes the novel's primary antagonist and anti-hero. His obsessive love for Catherine and systematic revenge against those who wronged him drives much of the plot. He represents the forces of nature, passion, and vengeance.
characters.physical: Black eyes, dark-skinned, 'gipsy in aspect,' erect and handsome figure, gentleman in dress and manners though somewhat slovenly. Later becomes taller, athletic, well-formed with military bearing. In death, appears to sneer with eyes open.
characters.personality: Passionate, vengeful, manipulative, intelligent, volatile, emotionally unstable, capable of both deep love and profound cruelty. Becomes increasingly obsessed and supernatural in his final days.
characters.background: Found starving in Liverpool streets by Mr. Earnshaw, brought to Wuthering Heights as a child. Degraded by Hindley after Mr. Earnshaw's death, disappears for three years and returns wealthy and educated. Marries Isabella Linton for revenge, systematically destroys his enemies.

relationships.description: Eternal, spiritual love that transcends death
Married for revenge against Edgar, treated cruelly
Systematically destroyed through gambling and drink
Hated as Catherine's husband and symbol of genteel society
Deliberately degraded as revenge against Hindley

characters.arc: Transforms from degraded foundling to gentleman to monster of revenge. His love for Catherine becomes destructive obsession that consumes his life. Finally finds peace in death, reunited with Catherine's spirit.

characters.name: Edgar Linton
characters.description: The refined, gentle master of Thrushcross Grange who marries Catherine Earnshaw. He represents civilization, culture, and restraint in contrast to Heathcliff's passionate nature. Despite his weakness, he shows genuine love and devotion.
characters.physical: Great blue eyes, even forehead, handsome with light hair and fair skin, slender and youth-like build. Uses cambric pocket-handkerchief. In death at age 39, looks ten years younger.
characters.personality: Refined, scholarly, gentle, non-violent by nature but can be provoked to action, loving but sometimes stubborn, devoted father and husband.
characters.background: Son of the wealthy Linton family at Thrushcross Grange. Well-educated and cultured. Marries Catherine despite her connection to Heathcliff. Dies relatively young, leaving daughter Catherine orphaned.

relationships.description: Genuinely loves her despite her divided heart
Protective but ultimately disowns her after elopement
Devoted, overprotective father
Civilized opponent in love triangle

characters.arc: Represents the civilized world's attempt to tame wild passion. Wins Catherine's hand but never her whole heart. Dies defeated by forces he cannot understand or control.

characters.name: Catherine Linton
characters.description: The daughter of Catherine Earnshaw and Edgar Linton, representing the next generation's potential for redemption. She initially shows her mother's pride and willfulness but learns to love genuinely through her relationship with Hareton.
characters.physical: Real beauty with Earnshaws' handsome dark eyes, Lintons' fair skin and small features, yellow curling hair, light shining ringlets, smiting beauty, small white hand. Resembles both parents.
characters.personality: Initially haughty, contemptuous, proud, but capable of growth. Shows both her mother's spirit and capacity for genuine affection. Learns humility and compassion.
characters.background: Born prematurely when her mother dies. Raised by Edgar at Thrushcross Grange in sheltered circumstances. Forced into marriage with Linton Heathcliff, then widowed. Eventually finds love with Hareton Earnshaw.

relationships.description: Beloved daughter, center of his world after Catherine's death
Initially contemptuous, later loving teacher and partner
Forced marriage, showed compassion despite his weakness
Defiant victim of his revenge schemes

characters.arc: Begins as spoiled, class-conscious child but grows into woman capable of true love and redemption. Her union with Hareton represents the healing of old wounds.

characters.name: Hareton Earnshaw
characters.description: The son of Hindley Earnshaw, deliberately degraded by Heathcliff as revenge. Despite his rough upbringing, he retains inherent nobility and goodness. His education and redemption through Catherine's love represents hope for the future.
characters.physical: Strong, well-made, athletic, handsome features, brown curls, great tall lad, decidedly shabby clothing initially but later respectably dressed. Resembles Catherine Earnshaw, especially the eyes.
characters.personality: Initially rough, hostile, and crude due to degraded upbringing, but reveals inherent nobility, pride in his name and heritage, capacity for loyalty and love, bashful around Catherine.
characters.background: Son of Hindley and Frances Earnshaw, rightful heir to Wuthering Heights. Deliberately kept ignorant and degraded by Heathcliff as revenge against his father. Learns to read and better himself through Catherine's teaching.

relationships.description: Deceased father whom he barely knew
Degraded by him but remains loyal
Initially hostile, becomes devoted pupil and lover
Strong physical resemblance, especially eyes

characters.arc: Represents the possibility of redemption and education overcoming degradation. His love story with Catherine shows that the sins of the fathers need not be visited on the children.

characters.name: Ellen Dean
characters.description: The primary narrator of the story, serving as housekeeper to both families. She provides a practical, moral perspective on the events, though she sometimes enables the drama through her actions. She represents common sense and conventional morality.
characters.physical: Described as having ruddy countenance, stout build, strong constitution. Nearly 45 years old during the main narrative.
characters.personality: Practical, observant, loyal, sometimes manipulative, protective of the children she helps raise, morally conventional but capable of bending rules for those she loves.
characters.background: Grew up at Wuthering Heights where her mother was nurse to Hindley. Serves both households throughout her life, witnessing all the major events of the story.

relationships.description: Nursed him as child, later serves his family
Helps raise her, serves as confidante
Primary caretaker after Edgar's death
Nursed him as baby and child

characters.arc: Serves as the moral center and witness to the entire saga. Her narration provides structure and perspective to the wild passions of the main characters.

characters.name: Hindley Earnshaw
characters.description: Catherine's older brother who becomes master of Wuthering Heights after their father's death. His jealousy and cruelty toward Heathcliff sets much of the tragedy in motion. He represents the destructive power of resentment and addiction.
characters.physical: Dark, square head as a child, later described as tall, gaunt man without neckerchief, extremely slovenly, features lost in masses of shaggy hair, eyes like ghostly Catherine's with beauty annihilated.
characters.personality: Cruel, jealous, tyrannical toward Heathcliff, increasingly dissolute and alcoholic after his wife's death, violent and unstable, capable of both love (for his wife) and hatred.
characters.background: Son of Mr. Earnshaw, sent to college to separate him from Heathcliff. Returns with mysterious wife Frances, becomes master of Wuthering Heights, degrades Heathcliff, gambles away estate, dies of alcoholism at 27.

relationships.description: Less favored son, sent away due to conflicts
Often in conflict, especially over Heathcliff
Childhood rival who becomes object of systematic degradation
Devoted to his mysterious wife until her death
Son he barely knows due to alcoholism

characters.arc: Transforms from jealous child to cruel master to self-destructive alcoholic. His degradation of Heathcliff boomerangs back to destroy his own family line.

characters.name: Isabella Linton
characters.description: Edgar's naive younger sister who becomes infatuated with Heathcliff and elopes with him, only to discover his true cruel nature. She represents the danger of romantic illusion and the vulnerability of innocence to predatory evil.
characters.physical: Charming young lady, eighteen years old, blonde hair and pale skin, infantile in manners, light silk dress inappropriate for rough conditions. Later appears injured and disheveled after abuse.
characters.personality: Naive, romantic, initially charmed by Heathcliff's dark appeal, becomes desperate and regretful, shows courage in eventually escaping, intelligent but inexperienced.
characters.background: Sheltered younger sister of Edgar Linton, becomes infatuated with Heathcliff despite warnings, elopes and suffers abuse, escapes and bears Heathcliff's son, dies twelve years later in the south.

relationships.description: Protective brother who disowns her after elopement
Abusive marriage, victim of his revenge plot
Bears and raises Heathcliff's weak son
Admired her, becomes rival for Heathcliff's attention

characters.arc: Falls from sheltered innocence into the hell of an abusive marriage, ultimately escapes but remains scarred. Her son inherits her weakness.

characters.name: Linton Heathcliff
characters.description: The sickly son of Heathcliff and Isabella, used as a pawn in his father's revenge schemes. He represents the corruption of innocence and the destructive effects of being raised without love.
characters.physical: Pale, delicate, effeminate boy resembling Edgar Linton but with sickly peevishness, flaxen hair, large languid blue eyes, extremely frail and feeble, thin face, attenuated fingers, nerveless frame.
characters.personality: Weak, sickly, complaining, manipulative, petulant, self-pitying, demanding, cowardly, cruel despite physical weakness, fearful of his father.
characters.background: Born to Isabella and Heathcliff, raised by his mother until age 12, brought to live with father at Wuthering Heights, used as tool to capture Catherine Linton in marriage, dies young after brief marriage.

relationships.description: Terrorized and manipulated by his cruel father
Raised by her in the south until her death
Forced marriage, manipulates her emotions
Brief period of kindness before forced to live with father

characters.arc: Represents the victimization of children by adult revenge. His weakness and cruelty show how loveless upbringing corrupts natural goodness.

characters.name: Mr. Earnshaw
characters.description: The patriarch of Wuthering Heights who brings the foundling Heathcliff into the family, setting the tragic events in motion. He represents misguided kindness that has far-reaching consequences.
characters.physical: Failing health in later years, confined to chimney-corner, weakened strength, strokes Catherine's hair before dying peacefully by the fireside.
characters.personality: Kind-hearted but sometimes severe, increasingly irritable in old age, painfully protective of Heathcliff, strict with his own children, doesn't understand jokes.
characters.background: Master of Wuthering Heights, father of Hindley and Catherine. Finds Heathcliff starving in Liverpool and brings him home. Dies peacefully while Catherine sings to him.

relationships.description: Less favored son, considers him worthless
Troubled by her wild behavior but loves her devotedly
Shows favoritism that breeds family resentment
Opposed his decision to bring home Heathcliff

characters.arc: Well-intentioned patriarch whose favoritism toward Heathcliff creates the conditions for future tragedy. Dies before seeing the consequences of his actions.

characters.name: Mr. Lockwood
characters.description: The frame narrator and tenant of Thrushcross Grange who listens to Nelly's story. He represents the outsider's perspective on the wild passions of Yorkshire, providing a civilized contrast to the main characters' extremes.
characters.physical: Self-described misanthropist, rides a horse, wears coat with coat-laps, genteel and educated appearance, recovers from illness during the story.
characters.personality: Reserved, analytical, socially awkward despite education, curious but somewhat passive, becomes frightened easily, considers himself sensible.
characters.background: Southern gentleman who rents Thrushcross Grange from Heathcliff. His curiosity about his strange neighbors leads him to hear their complete history from Nelly Dean.

relationships.description: Rents property from him, fascinated and repelled by his nature
His housekeeper who tells him the family history
Briefly considers romantic interest

characters.arc: Serves as civilized observer of passionate extremes. His conventional nature provides contrast to the wild emotions of the main story.

characters.name: Joseph
characters.description: The elderly, fanatically religious servant at Wuthering Heights who survives through all the family changes. He represents rigid religious hypocrisy and the persistence of the old order despite all upheavals.
characters.physical: Very old but hale and sinewy, elderly man with lantern jaws, wears sanctimonious and sour expression, smokes black pipe, speaks in heavy Yorkshire dialect.
characters.personality: Religious zealot, self-righteous Pharisee, harsh toward children, speaks in dialect, contemptuous, rude, deaf or deliberately ignoring, moralistic but crude.
characters.background: Long-term servant at Wuthering Heights who serves through multiple generations. Gains influence over the weakening Mr. Earnshaw, survives all the family changes and conflicts.

relationships.description: Manipulates weakening master against the children
Serves him but maintains independent moral judgments
Instills pride of lineage but contributes to his degradation

characters.arc: Represents the unchanging, bigoted old order that persists through all the passionate changes around him. His survival emphasizes the continuity of place despite human upheaval.

locations.name: Wuthering Heights
locations.description: The ancient farmhouse stronghold of the Earnshaw family, built in 1500 as indicated by the carved inscription above the door. A fortress-like dwelling exposed to stormy weather, built strong with narrow deep-set windows, large jutting stones at corners, and grotesque carving on the front. The main room serves as kitchen and parlour with huge fireplace, oak dresser, smooth white stone floor, and weapons displayed above the chimney.
locations.significance: Represents the wild, natural forces of passion and revenge. Home to the Earnshaw family and later controlled by Heathcliff as the center of his revenge operations. The harsh, primitive setting reflects the violent emotions of its inhabitants.

locations.name: Thrushcross Grange
locations.description: The elegant, refined home of the Linton family, larger and more comfortable than Wuthering Heights. Features a library, drawing-room with crimson carpet and furniture, parlour with windows overlooking gardens, and multiple bedrooms. Surrounded by a park with formal walking paths, buried in trees, representing civilization and culture.
locations.significance: Represents civilization, culture, and social refinement in contrast to Wuthering Heights' primitive wildness. The civilized world that Catherine Earnshaw enters through marriage, and later becomes the inheritance battleground for the next generation.

locations.name: The Moors
locations.description: The wild, open landscape surrounding both houses, characterized by heath, bilberry plants, rough hills, and exposed terrain. Features guide-stones marking paths between locations, temporary brooks during storms, and areas where characters meet for secret encounters.
locations.significance: Represents the wild, untamed nature that calls to Catherine's true self. The neutral ground between the two houses where characters often meet, and symbolic of the freedom that Catherine yearns for throughout her life.

locations.name: Gimmerton Kirk
locations.description: The village church and churchyard where the Earnshaw and Linton families are buried. Features scattered gravestones, two fir-trees in the yard, and Catherine Earnshaw's unusual grave in the corner where the wall is low and heath plants climb over from the moor.
locations.significance: The final resting place that becomes central to the novel's supernatural elements. Catherine's burial in the corner rather than with either family reflects her divided nature. Site of Heathcliff's obsessive visits and supernatural encounters.

locations.name: Penistone Crags
locations.description: Rocky outcrops about a mile and a half beyond Wuthering Heights, featuring rough terrain where Joseph loads lime. A destination for adventurous walks and exploration, representing the wild landscape beyond civilized boundaries.
locations.significance: Represents the ultimate wild freedom that Catherine dreams of reaching. The landscape beyond even Wuthering Heights, symbolizing complete escape from social constraints.

locations.name: The Guide-Stone
locations.description: Stone marker at crossroads with carved letters indicating directions: 'W.H.' for Wuthering Heights, 'G.' for Gimmerton village, and 'T.G.' for Thrushcross Grange. Contains a hole filled with snail-shells and pebbles, marking the intersection of the three main locations.
locations.significance: Symbolic center point representing the intersection of different worlds and choices. Site of nostalgic memories and planned meetings between the younger generation.
